what: Comparing methods on a simulated series
title: Comparing changepoint methods with ggchangepoint
text: Generate three-regime series changes mean variance: faceted layout shows method panel, x-axes aligned:  overlay layout draws changepoints single panel, colour-coded:  numeric summary, ggcpt_compare_table() returns tidy tibble:
code: set.seed(2022) x <- c(rnorm(100, 0, 1), rnorm(100, 10, 1), rnorm(100, 5, 2)) ggcpt_compare(x, methods = cmp_methods, layout = "facet") ggcpt_compare(x, methods = cmp_methods, layout = "overlay") ggcpt_compare_table(x, methods = cmp_methods) #> # A tibble: 10 × 3 #>    method    cp cp_value #>    <chr>  <dbl>    <dbl> #>  1 pelt     100    0.467 #>  2 pelt     200   10.3   #>  3 binseg   100    0.467 #>  4 binseg   200   10.3   #>  5 fpop     100    0.467 #>  6 fpop     200   10.3   #>  7 fpop     247    3.83  #>  8 fpop     251    8.22  #>  9 fpop     284    2.81  #> 10 fpop     287    8.76

title: Comparing changepoint methods with ggchangepoint
text: true changepoint locations known, cpt_metrics() provides standard suite accuracy measures (Truong et al. 2020; van den Burg Williams 2020):
code: truth <- c(100, 200)  # PELT res_pelt <- cpt_detect(x, method = "pelt", change_in = "meanvar") generics::tidy(res_pelt)$cp #> [1] 100 200  # Metrics for BinSeg res_binseg <- cpt_detect(x, method = "binseg", change_in = "meanvar") cpt_metrics(generics::tidy(res_binseg)$cp, truth, n = length(x)) #> # A tibble: 1 × 12 #>       n n_pred n_truth precision recall    f1 covering hausdorff rand_index #>   <int>  <int>   <int>     <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1   300      2       2         1      1     1        1         0          1 #> # ℹ 3 more variables: annotation_error <int>, mae_matched <dbl>, #> #   rmse_matched <dbl>

text: now conduct systematic comparison across five built-test signals (Donoho Johnstone 1994), using tolerance margin 5 observations. table shows different methods perform across signal types. title: Comparing changepoint methods with ggchangepoint
text: Precision / Recall / F1: standard classification measures margin tolerance. Covering: average Jaccard overlap true predicted segments (van den Burg Williams 2020). Hausdorff distance: worst-case localisation error predicted true changepoint sets.

title: Comparing changepoint methods with ggchangepoint
text: complete reproducible workflow combining simulator, detection, evaluation:
code: set.seed(1) dat <- cpt_simulate(200, changepoints = c(60, 120),                     change_in = "mean", params = c(0, 8, 3), sd = 1) truth <- attr(dat, "true_changepoints")  res <- cpt_detect(dat$value, method = "pelt", change_in = "mean") pred <- generics::tidy(res)$cp  cpt_metrics(pred, truth, n = length(dat$value)) #> # A tibble: 1 × 12 #>       n n_pred n_truth precision recall    f1 covering hausdorff rand_index #>   <int>  <int>   <int>     <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1   200      2       2         1      1     1        1         0          1 #> # ℹ 3 more variables: annotation_error <int>, mae_matched <dbl>, #> #   rmse_matched <dbl>

text: multiple ground-truth annotations available—Turing Change Point Dataset (van den Burg Williams 2020)—use cpt_metrics_annotated() average metrics across annotators:
code: cpt_metrics_annotated(pred = c(100, 200),                       annotations = list(c(100, 200), c(105, 198)),                       n = 300) #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>       n n_annotators n_pred precision recall    f1 covering #>   <dbl>        <int>  <int>     <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1   300            2      2         1      1     1    0.977

title: Comparing changepoint methods with ggchangepoint
text: vignette("introduction", package = "ggchangepoint") comprehensive walkthrough package API supported methods. R package references individual detection engines: changepoint (Killick Eckley 2014), wbs (Fryzlewicz 2014), (Baranowski et al. 2019), mosum (Eichinger Kirch 2018), fpop (Maidstone et al. 2017), IDetect (Anastasiou Fryzlewicz 2022), breakfast (Fryzlewicz 2020, 2022), changepoint.np (Haynes et al. 2017), ecp (James Matteson 2014).

title: ggchangepoint: A Unified Tidy Interface for Changepoint Analysis in R
text: ggchangepoint R package provides unified, tidy interface changepoint detection across multiple algorithmic backends. introduces ggcpt S3 result class broom-style methods (tidy(), glance(), augment()), central cpt_detect() dispatcher supporting dozen detection algorithms, native ggplot2 visualization via autoplot() specialised geoms, method comparison accuracy evaluation modules, data simulation framework canonical test signals. harmonising disparate APIs existing R changepoint packages behind single convention, ggchangepoint lowers barrier exploratory changepoint analysis reproducible method comparison.

title: ggchangepoint: A Unified Tidy Interface for Changepoint Analysis in R
text: Changepoint detection—problem identifying points sequence underlying statistical properties change—fundamental task time series analysis (Truong et al. 2020; Aminikhanghahi Cook 2017). applications across virtually every domain involves sequential data, including genomics (Picard et al. 2005), finance (Athey et al. 2022), climate science (Haslett Raftery 1989), signal processing (Lavielle 2005). R ecosystem offers rich set changepoint packages, implementing one detection algorithms conventions input, output, parameterisation. changepoint package (Killick Eckley 2014) provides PELT (Killick et al. 2012), Binary Segmentation (Scott Knott 1974; Vostrikova 1981), Segmented Neighbourhood, AMOC. wbs (Fryzlewicz 2014) breakfast packages implement Wild Binary Segmentation variants, (Baranowski et al. 2019), mosum (Eichinger Kirch 2018), fpop (Maidstone et al. 2017), IDetect (Anastasiou Fryzlewicz 2022), others offer specialised algorithms. nonparametric side, changepoint.np (Haynes et al. 2017) ecp (James Matteson 2014; Matteson James 2014) handle distributional changes. diversity strength R community, creates practical difficulties analyst. package uses different result class, different naming conventions parameters, different plot methods, different changepoint indexing conventions. Comparing output several detectors data—standard practice robust analysis—requires user write manual conversion code. Furthermore, none existing packages natively produce ggplot2 (Wickham 2016) graphics support broom (Robinson 2017) convention tidy data extraction. ggchangepoint addresses problems providing single, consistent interface wraps widely used detection packages. design goals : Uniformity: single ggcpt result class regardless underlying detection engine, broom-style methods tidy data access. Discoverability: central cpt_detect() dispatcher whose documentation lists supported methods capabilities. Visualisation: first-class ggplot2 integration autoplot() specialised geoms. Comparison evaluation: built-tools running multiple detectors, tabulating results, computing accuracy metrics, visualising discrepancies. Reproducibility: simulation framework generates data known changepoints, enabling rigorous benchmarking.

title: ggchangepoint: A Unified Tidy Interface for Changepoint Analysis in R
text: ggcpt class S3 class stores complete output changepoint detection structured format. Every detection function ggchangepoint—whether called cpt_detect() directly—returns ggcpt object, ensuring uniform interface downstream processing. ggcpt object named list following components: changepoints: tibble detected changepoint locations (cp) corresponding data values (cp_value). segments: tibble describing fitted segments (segment ID, start, end, length, segment-level parameter estimate). data: original data series tidy tibble index value. metadata fields: method, change_in, penalty, fit (raw upstream object), call, cp_convention. cp_convention component records whether changepoint indices follow left-segment convention—last observation change, used changepoint package—right-segment convention. ggchangepoint methods report locations left-segment convention, results packages use alternative convention (e.g., ecp) normalised automatically methods can compared common footing.
code: library(ggchangepoint) library(ggplot2) library(generics) theme_set(theme_light())  set.seed(2022) x <- c(rnorm(100, 0, 1), rnorm(100, 10, 1))  res <- cpt_detect(x, method = "pelt", change_in = "mean") class(res) #> [1] "ggcpt" print(res) #> ggcpt (changepoint detection result) #>   Method:          pelt  #>   Change in:       mean  #>   Changepoints found: 1  #>   CP convention:   left  #>   Penalty:         MBIC = NA  #>   Series length:   200  #>  #> Changepoints: #> # A tibble: 1 × 2 #>      cp cp_value #>   <int>    <dbl> #> 1   100    0.467 res$cp_convention #> [1] "left"

title: ggchangepoint: A Unified Tidy Interface for Changepoint Analysis in R
text: Following broom convention (Robinson 2017) standardised data access, ggcpt objects support tidy(), glance(), augment(). tidy() returns changepoint locations tibble, one row per changepoint: glance() returns one-row summary series length, number detected changepoints, method, change type, penalty information: augment() returns original data augmented segment identifiers, fitted segment-level parameter estimates, residuals, logical flag indicating changepoint positions: methods make straightforward pipe ggchangepoint results analysis custom visualisation.
code: generics::tidy(res) #> # A tibble: 1 × 2 #>      cp cp_value #>   <int>    <dbl> #> 1   100    0.467 generics::glance(res) #> # A tibble: 1 × 9 #>       n n_changepoints method change_in penalty_type penalty_value cp_convention #>   <int>          <int> <chr>  <chr>     <chr>                <dbl> <chr>         #> 1   200              1 pelt   mean      MBIC                    NA left          #> # ℹ 2 more variables: total_cost <dbl>, runtime <dbl> generics::augment(res) #> # A tibble: 200 × 6 #>    index  value seg_id .fitted .resid is_changepoint #>    <int>  <dbl>  <int>   <dbl>  <dbl> <lgl>          #>  1     1  0.900      1   0.139  0.761 FALSE          #>  2     2 -1.17       1   0.139 -1.31  FALSE          #>  3     3 -0.897      1   0.139 -1.04  FALSE          #>  4     4 -1.44       1   0.139 -1.58  FALSE          #>  5     5 -0.331      1   0.139 -0.470 FALSE          #>  6     6 -2.90       1   0.139 -3.04  FALSE          #>  7     7 -1.06       1   0.139 -1.20  FALSE          #>  8     8  0.278      1   0.139  0.139 FALSE          #>  9     9  0.749      1   0.139  0.611 FALSE          #> 10    10  0.242      1   0.139  0.103 FALSE          #> # ℹ 190 more rows

title: ggchangepoint: A Unified Tidy Interface for Changepoint Analysis in R
text: cpt_detect() function serves primary entry point changepoint detection. accepts data series, method name, change type, optional penalty parameters, dispatches appropriate backend wrapper: following detection methods currently supported:
code: cpt_detect(x, method = "pelt", change_in = "mean") #> ggcpt (changepoint detection result) #>   Method:          pelt  #>   Change in:       mean  #>   Changepoints found: 1  #>   CP convention:   left  #>   Penalty:         MBIC = NA  #>   Series length:   200  #>  #> Changepoints: #> # A tibble: 1 × 2 #>      cp cp_value #>   <int>    <dbl> #> 1   100    0.467 cpt_detect(x, method = "fpop", change_in = "mean") #> ggcpt (changepoint detection result) #>   Method:          fpop  #>   Change in:       mean  #>   Changepoints found: 1  #>   CP convention:   left  #>   Penalty:         Manual = 10.5966347330961  #>   Series length:   200  #>  #> Changepoints: #> # A tibble: 1 × 2 #>      cp cp_value #>   <int>    <dbl> #> 1   100    0.467

title: ggchangepoint: A Unified Tidy Interface for Changepoint Analysis in R
text: methods support (PELT, Binary Segmentation, Segmented Neighbourhood, AMOC, FPOP), penalty parameter can controlled via cpt_penalty() penalty argument. Penalties can information criteria—"BIC" (Yao 1988), "AIC"—user-specified numeric values:
code: cpt_detect(x, method = "pelt", change_in = "mean", penalty = "BIC") #> ggcpt (changepoint detection result) #>   Method:          pelt  #>   Change in:       mean  #>   Changepoints found: 1  #>   CP convention:   left  #>   Penalty:         BIC = NA  #>   Series length:   200  #>  #> Changepoints: #> # A tibble: 1 × 2 #>      cp cp_value #>   <int>    <dbl> #> 1   100    0.467 cpt_detect(x, method = "fpop", change_in = "mean", penalty = 2 * log(200)) #> ggcpt (changepoint detection result) #>   Method:          fpop  #>   Change in:       mean  #>   Changepoints found: 1  #>   CP convention:   left  #>   Penalty:         Manual = 10.5966347330961  #>   Series length:   200  #>  #> Changepoints: #> # A tibble: 1 × 2 #>      cp cp_value #>   <int>    <dbl> #> 1   100    0.467

title: ggchangepoint: A Unified Tidy Interface for Changepoint Analysis in R
text: users wish build custom visualisations, ggchangepoint provides four new geoms stats. geom_changepoint() adds vertical lines changepoint positions:  geom_cpt_segment() draws fitted segment-level means changepoints:  stat_changepoint() runs cpt_detect() inline within ggplot pipeline:  geom_cpt_ci() adds confidence intervals around segment estimates.
code: ggplot(data.frame(t = seq_along(x), y = x), aes(t, y)) +   geom_line() +   geom_changepoint(data = generics::tidy(res), aes(xintercept = cp)) seg <- res$segments ggplot(data.frame(t = seq_along(x), y = x), aes(t, y)) +   geom_line() +   geom_cpt_segment(data = seg,                    aes(x = start, xend = end, y = param_estimate,                        yend = param_estimate),                    colour = "steelblue", linewidth = 1.2) ggplot(data.frame(t = seq_along(x), y = x), aes(t, y)) +   geom_line() +   stat_changepoint(method = "pelt", change_in = "mean")

title: ggchangepoint: A Unified Tidy Interface for Changepoint Analysis in R
text: ggcpt_compare() runs several methods arranges results either facetted panels (one per method) overlay colour-coded changepoint markers:   numeric summary, ggcpt_compare_table() returns tidy tibble detected changepoints across methods:
code: x3 <- c(rnorm(100, 0, 1), rnorm(100, 10, 1), rnorm(100, 5, 2)) cmp_methods <- if (has_fpop) c("pelt", "binseg", "fpop") else c("pelt", "binseg", "amoc")  ggcpt_compare(x3, methods = cmp_methods, layout = "facet") ggcpt_compare(x3, methods = cmp_methods, layout = "overlay") ggcpt_compare_table(x3, methods = cmp_methods) #> # A tibble: 10 × 3 #>    method    cp cp_value #>    <chr>  <dbl>    <dbl> #>  1 pelt     100    1.47  #>  2 pelt     200    9.94  #>  3 pelt     214    7.52  #>  4 pelt     222    0.581 #>  5 binseg   100    1.47  #>  6 binseg   200    9.94  #>  7 fpop     100    1.47  #>  8 fpop     200    9.94  #>  9 fpop     214    7.52  #> 10 fpop     222    0.581

title: ggchangepoint: A Unified Tidy Interface for Changepoint Analysis in R
text: many methods compared, ggcpt_compare() respects future::plan() parallelisation strategy future future.apply packages available. Detection fanned requested methods; supplying seed makes parallel run reproducible via parallel-safe L’Ecuyer-CMRG streams:
code: future::plan(future::multisession, workers = 2) ggcpt_compare(x, methods = c("pelt", "binseg", "fpop", "wbs", "not"),               seed = 1)

title: ggchangepoint: A Unified Tidy Interface for Changepoint Analysis in R
text: ground-truth changepoint locations known—either synthetic data labelled data set—ggchangepoint provides comprehensive suite accuracy metrics cpt_metrics(): Precision recall based margin tolerance (default 5). F1 score, harmonic mean precision recall. Covering metric, length-weighted average Jaccard overlap true predicted segmentations (van den Burg Williams 2020). Hausdorff distance sets predicted true changepoint locations. Adjusted Rand index, measuring agreement induced segment labellings (Hariz et al. 2007). Annotation error, absolute difference predicted true number changepoints (van den Burg Williams 2020). Mean absolute error (MAE) root mean squared error (RMSE) changepoint locations. tolerance margin, detections within margin considered correct: scenarios multiple ground-truth annotations, cpt_metrics_annotated() computes metrics annotator averages: ggcpt_eval() function provides visual evaluation plot, showing true predicted changepoints colour-coded match status:
code: cpt_metrics(pred = c(100, 200), truth = c(100, 200), n = 300) #> # A tibble: 1 × 12 #>       n n_pred n_truth precision recall    f1 covering hausdorff rand_index #>   <int>  <int>   <int>     <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1   300      2       2         1      1     1        1         0          1 #> # ℹ 3 more variables: annotation_error <int>, mae_matched <dbl>, #> #   rmse_matched <dbl> cpt_metrics(pred = c(105, 205), truth = c(100, 200), n = 300, margin = 10) #> # A tibble: 1 × 12 #>       n n_pred n_truth precision recall    f1 covering hausdorff rand_index #>   <int>  <int>   <int>     <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1   300      2       2         1      1     1    0.936         5      0.903 #> # ℹ 3 more variables: annotation_error <int>, mae_matched <dbl>, #> #   rmse_matched <dbl> cpt_metrics_annotated(pred = c(100, 200),                       annotations = list(c(100, 200), c(105, 198)),                       n = 300) #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>       n n_annotators n_pred precision recall    f1 covering #>   <dbl>        <int>  <int>     <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1   300            2      2         1      1     1    0.977 ggcpt_eval(pred = c(100, 200), truth = c(100, 200), data_vec = x)

text: Reproducible synthetic data essential benchmarking detection algorithms. ggchangepoint provides cpt_simulate() (shorthand rcpt()) generating time series known changepoint locations across range scenarios.

title: ggchangepoint: A Unified Tidy Interface for Changepoint Analysis in R
text: simulator supports changes mean, variance, , slope, four noise models—Gaussian, Student-t, AR(1), random walk: true changepoint locations stored attribute:
code: seg_params <- list(   list(mean = 0, sd = 1),   list(mean = 10, sd = 1),   list(mean = 5, sd = 0.5),   list(mean = -2, sd = 1) ) dat <- cpt_simulate(200, changepoints = c(50, 100, 150),                     change_in = "meanvar",                     params = seg_params) attr(dat, "true_changepoints") #> [1]  50 100 150

title: ggchangepoint: A Unified Tidy Interface for Changepoint Analysis in R
text: package also includes five canonical test signals adapted wavelet changepoint literature (Donoho Johnstone 1994): signal known changepoint locations suitable benchmarking detection accuracy across different signal structures.
code: blocks  <- signal_blocks(512) fms     <- signal_fms(512) mix     <- signal_mix(512) teeth   <- signal_teeth(512) stairs  <- signal_stairs(512)

what: Case Study: Comparative Evaluation on a Block Signal
title: ggchangepoint: A Unified Tidy Interface for Changepoint Analysis in R
text: illustrate complete workflow—simulation, detection, comparison, evaluation—using Blocks test signal added Gaussian noise: Detect changepoints every method available build, score known truth tolerance margin 5, collect results single table: Visual evaluation PELT result, ±5\pm 5 tolerance windows shaded predictions coloured match status:
code: set.seed(1) sig <- signal_blocks(512) truth <- attr(sig, "true_changepoints")  x_noisy <- sig$value + rnorm(512, 0, 0.5) methods_cs <- c("pelt", "binseg", "amoc") if (has_fpop) methods_cs <- c(methods_cs, "fpop") if (has_wbs)  methods_cs <- c(methods_cs, "wbs") if (has_not)  methods_cs <- c(methods_cs, "not")  metrics <- do.call(rbind, lapply(methods_cs, function(m) {   res  <- cpt_detect(x_noisy, method = m, change_in = "mean")   pred <- generics::tidy(res)$cp   data.frame(method = m, cpt_metrics(pred, truth, n = 512, margin = 5)) })) metrics[, c("method", "n_pred", "precision", "recall", "f1", "covering")] #>   method n_pred precision     recall        f1  covering #> 1   pelt      1         1 0.09090909 0.1666667 0.2378708 #> 2 binseg      1         1 0.09090909 0.1666667 0.2378708 #> 3   amoc      1         1 0.09090909 0.1666667 0.2378708 #> 4   fpop      1         1 0.09090909 0.1666667 0.2378708 #> 5    wbs      1         1 0.09090909 0.1666667 0.2378708 #> 6    not      1         1 0.09090909 0.1666667 0.2378708 pred_pelt <- generics::tidy(cpt_detect(x_noisy, method = "pelt"))$cp ggcpt_eval(pred = pred_pelt, truth = truth, data_vec = x_noisy)

title: ggchangepoint: A Unified Tidy Interface for Changepoint Analysis in R
text: ggchangepoint provides unified, tidy interface diverse changepoint detection ecosystem R. standardising single result class, adopting broom conventions, integrating natively ggplot2, package reduces friction exploratory changepoint analysis facilitates reproducible method comparison. Planned directions future development include: Additional wrapper coverage: integration detection packages strucchange, segmented, bcp. Online detection: support streaming sequential changepoint detection. Model selection helpers: visual tools penalty selection, including elbow plots cross-validated loss curves. Multivariate high-dimensional methods: improved handling multivariate changepoint detection, leveraging ecp’s existing multi-dimensional support. Interactive visualisation: integration interactive plotting frameworks exploratory data analysis. Contributions bug reports welcome package’s GitHub repository (https://github.com/PursuitOfDataScience/ggchangepoint).

title: ggchangepoint: A Roadmap for the Next Release
text: ggchangepoint 0.1.0 combines three changepoint engines — changepoint, changepoint.np, ecp — behind small tidyverse-flavoured API (cpt_wrapper(), ecp_wrapper()) renders detected changepoints ggplot2 (ggcptplot(), ggecpplot()). document proposes substantial expansion package, grounded modern changepoint-detection literature, preserving two design commitments define package: () every detector returns tidy tibble, (ii) every result directly renderable ggplot2. survey impactful methodological literature — penalised optimal partitioning pruning (Optimal Partitioning, PELT, FPOP/SNIP, gfpop), randomised multiscale search (Binary Segmentation, WBS, WBS2/SDLL, , MOSUM, Isolate-Detect, TGUH), multiscale inference confidence (SMUCE/HSMUCE, FDRSeg), nonparametric distribution-free detection (NMCD, energy/E-Divisive, kernel/KCP, sequential CPMs), robustness dependence (biweight/robust loss, DeCAFS, self-normalisation), high-dimensional multivariate detection (inspect, sparsified binary segmentation, changepoints collection), Bayesian detection (product-partition bcp, Bayesian Online Changepoint Detection), structural breaks regression (Bai–Perron, strucchange, segmented), online/sequential monitoring (CPM, FOCuS, OCD). family specify mathematics, R package wrap, proposed tidy function signature, proposed visualisation. propose: unified ggcpt S3 result object broom-style tidy()/glance()/augment() methods ggplot2::autoplot() method; family new geoms/stats (geom_changepoint(), geom_cpt_segment(), geom_cpt_ci()); penalty-path / model-selection toolkit (CROPS, elbow solution-path plots); evaluation module implementing standard accuracy metrics (precision/recall/F1 margin, covering metric, Hausdorff distance, (adjusted) Rand index, annotation error) including multi-annotator support; data-generating module canonical benchmark signals. also audit four functions already shipped 0.1.0 specify concrete fixes (§12): confirmed edge-case bug ecp_wrapper() emits spurious boundary “changepoints” NA series change, live ggplot2 size→linewidth deprecation plot functions, --one inconsistency two detectors’ location conventions, absent input validation — scheduled v0.2.0. document closes phased release plan, dependency architecture notes, complete, verified reference list.

title: ggchangepoint: A Roadmap for the Next Release
text: useful taxonomy — structures §5 — distinguishes methods along axes: changes: mean / variance / mean+variance / slope (trend) / whole distribution / regression coefficients / covariance / network structure. Search strategy: exact dynamic programming (Optimal Partitioning, Segment Neighbourhood) vs. pruned exact (PELT, FPOP) vs. greedy/approximate (Binary Segmentation) vs. randomised (WBS, ) vs. moving-window (MOSUM) vs. isolation (Isolate-Detect). Parametric vs. nonparametric. Frequentist vs. Bayesian. Offline (batch) vs. online (sequential). Univariate vs. multivariate vs. high-dimensional. Independence vs. dependence/autocorrelation; light- vs. heavy-tailed noise.

previous_headings: 5. New detection methods > 5.1 Penalised optimal partitioning and functional pruning
title: ggchangepoint: A Roadmap for the Next Release
text: Optimal Partitioning (Jackson et al., 2005) solves penalised-cost problem exactly recursion F(t)=min0≤s<t{F(s)+𝒞(y(s+1):t)+β},F(0)=−β, F(t) = \min_{0 \le s < t} \Big\{ F(s) + \mathcal{C}(y_{(s+1):t}) + \beta \Big\}, \qquad F(0) = -\beta, O(n2)O(n^2). Segment Neighbourhood (Auger & Lawrence, 1989) instead fixes number segments O(Qn2)O(Q n^2) QQ changepoints. already reachable cpt_wrapper(cp_method = "SegNeigh"); document relationship PELT explicitly vignette.

previous_headings: 5. New detection methods > 5.1 Penalised optimal partitioning and functional pruning
title: ggchangepoint: A Roadmap for the Next Release
text: PELT (Killick, Fearnhead & Eckley, 2012) augments Optimal Partitioning pruning step: candidate last-changepoint ss can discarded permanently F(s)+𝒞(y(s+1):t)+K>F(t), F(s) + \mathcal{C}(y_{(s+1):t}) + K \;>\; F(t), provided exists constant KK 𝒞(y(s+1):t)+𝒞(y(t+1):T)+K≤𝒞(y(s+1):T)\mathcal{C}(y_{(s+1):t}) + \mathcal{C}(y_{(t+1):T}) + K \le \mathcal{C}(y_{(s+1):T}) T>tT>t. assumption changepoints spread throughout data, PELT achieves expected O(n)O(n) cost remaining exact. default engine cpt_wrapper(); next release adds explanatory derivation runtime comparison plot vignette.

FPOP exact, returns segmentation PELT, empirically faster — crucially, speed robust number changepoints. SNIP companion segment-neighbourhood (fixed-QQ) formulation. Wraps: fpop (Fpop()). Proposed: fpop_wrapper(x, penalty = "BIC", ...) → ggcpt.

text: energy-distance methods Matteson & James (2014), implemented ecp, detect changes distribution multivariate data arbitrary dimension using divergence ℰ(X,Y;α)=2mn∑=1m∑j=1n‖Xi−Yj‖α−(m2)−1∑<k‖Xi−Xk‖α−(n2)−1∑j<l‖Yj−Yl‖α,α∈(0,2). \mathcal{E}(X, Y; \alpha) = \frac{2}{mn}\sum_{=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\lVert X_i - Y_j\rVert^\alpha - \binom{m}{2}^{-1}\!\!\sum_{<k}\lVert X_i - X_k\rVert^\alpha - \binom{n}{2}^{-1}\!\!\sum_{j<l}\lVert Y_j - Y_l\rVert^\alpha, \quad \alpha \(0,2). e.divisive adds changepoints bisection permutation test; e.agglo merges. next release lifts current univariate-treatment genuine multivariate input (see §5.6 §7.5) exposes alpha, sig.lvl, R (permutations).

previous_headings: 5. New detection methods > 5.5 Robustness and dependence
title: ggchangepoint: A Roadmap for the Next Release
text: Classical Gaussian costs inflate changepoint count data contain outliers heavy tails. Fearnhead & Rigaill (2019) show bounded loss functions robust arbitrarily extreme outliers, give exact dynamic program minimising penalised cost built bounded segment loss, 𝒞(ya:b)=minμ∑=abγ(yi−μσ),γ(u)=min⁡(u2,K2), \mathcal{C}(y_{:b}) = \min_{\mu} \sum_{=}^{b} \gamma\!\left(\frac{y_i - \mu}{\sigma}\right), \qquad \gamma(u) = \min\!\left(u^2,\, K^2\right), truncated-quadratic γ\gamma shown capping point’s influence K2K^2. robseg implementation provides bounded (biweight-style) loss together Huber L1L_1 variants heavy-tailed noise, consistently recovering number locations changes despite contamination. Wraps: robseg (Rob_seg.std()). Proposed: robseg_wrapper(x, loss = c("biweight","huber","L1","L2"), lambda = ..., ...).

previous_headings: 5. New detection methods > 5.5 Robustness and dependence
title: ggchangepoint: A Roadmap for the Next Release
text: DeCAFS (Romano, Rigaill, Runge & Fearnhead, 2022) targets common situation mean drifts (random-walk component) abrupt changes noise autocorrelated (AR(1)). Naïve change--mean methods badly -count regime. DeCAFS minimises penalised cost form ∑t[(yt−μt)2/σ2+(μt−μt−1)2/η2⋅𝟙{change}]+β⋅(number changes), \sum_{t} \Big[ (y_t - \mu_t)^2/\sigma^2 + (\mu_t - \mu_{t-1})^2/\eta^2 \cdot \mathbb{1}\{\text{change}\} \Big] + \beta \cdot (\text{number changes}), efficient dynamic program copes cross-segment dependence. Wraps: DeCAFS (DeCAFS(), estimateParameters()). Proposed: decafs_wrapper(x, beta = ..., model_param = NULL, ...).

previous_headings: 5. New detection methods > 5.7 Bayesian changepoint detection
title: ggchangepoint: A Roadmap for the Next Release
text: BOCPD (Adams & MacKay, 2007) maintains, online, posterior run length rtr_t (time since last changepoint) via message passing recursion P(rt,y1:t)=∑rt−1P(rt∣rt−1)P(yt∣rt−1,yt(r))P(rt−1,y1:t−1), P(r_t, y_{1:t}) = \sum_{r_{t-1}} P(r_t \mid r_{t-1})\, P(y_t \mid r_{t-1}, y^{(r)}_{t})\, P(r_{t-1}, y_{1:t-1}), hazard function H(⋅)H(\cdot) governing P(rt∣rt−1)P(r_t \mid r_{t-1}) conjugate predictive per-segment model (e.g. Gaussian, Poisson). ocp package implements Gaussian user-defined predictives. Wraps: ocp (onlineCPD()). Proposed: bocpd_wrapper(x, hazard_lambda = 250, ...). Plot: run-length posterior heatmap (§7.7) — signature BOCPD graphic.

title: ggchangepoint: A Roadmap for the Next Release
text: distinguish three regimes: Orchestration-level (embarrassingly parallel; code; immediate win). Loops package drives independent can farmed directly: ggcpt_compare() running K methods one series (§7.3); batch/panel detection — one univariate detector applied many series, p coordinates matrix (ggcpt_facet, §7.5); Monte Carlo replications method×penalty grids evaluation simulation modules (§8, §9) — usually single largest time sink methodological work; coarse penalty grid (using purpose-built, computation-reusing CROPS §6); permutation/bootstrap replicates drive . Engine-internal (needs upstream support reimplementation; later). Inside single detector call often parallelism upstream C/C++ code expose: per-candidate segment-cost evaluations PELT/OP/FPOP dynamic programs, nquantiles empirical-CDF terms nonparametric cost cpt.np, pairwise energy-distance matrix permutation loop inside ecp::e.divisive(), M random-interval contrasts WBS/. real opportunities live API; treat upstream feature requests , warranted, candidates parallel re-implementation hot loop (e.g. via RcppParallel). Inherently sequential (parallelize). dynamic-programming recurrence — F(t)F(t) depends F(s),s<tF(s),\,s<t — online stream updates FOCuS/OCD/BOCPD sequential construction. parallel axis across problems (many streams, many series), never within one. separate, free win multithreaded linear algebra: SVD/eigen work inspect several changepoints estimators runs BLAS, multithreaded BLAS (OpenBLAS/MKL — cluster ships BLAS-enabled --openblas R modules) speeds code change.

what: 10.4.3 Infrastructure: a backend-agnostic layer over future
title: ggchangepoint: A Roadmap for the Next Release
text: standardise future ecosystem — future.apply::future_lapply() furrr::future_map() — rather calling parallel::mclapply() foreach directly, fits package’s users (HPC included — environment module/scheduler-based): One API, backend. user chooses parallelise single future::plan(); code written . plan(sequential) (default), plan(multisession) (PSOCK workers, platforms), plan(multicore) (fork, Unix/macOS -RStudio), plan(cluster, workers = ...) (sockets across nodes), future.batchtools / future.callr Slurm/SGE schedulers. package never hard-codes backend core count. platform traps. default sequential, nothing forks unexpectedly; users opt . honour ambient plan() expose light parallel/workers convenience sets plan locally restores exit. Progress interrupts. progressr gives backend-agnostic progress bars long studies; future propagates errors interrupts cleanly. Backends future/future.apply/furrr/progressr go Suggests; sequential path adds hard dependency (P1, §10.1).

text: many methods stochastic — ecp permutations, WBS/random intervals, cpt.np quantile sampling, mosum/kcpRS resampling, §9 simulator — naïve parallelism silently destroy reproducibility: single set.seed() propagate workers, ordinary RNG streams overlap across processes. package must therefore use parallel-safe L’Ecuyer-CMRG streams: future.apply/furrr provide via future.seed = TRUE (furrr_options(seed = TRUE)), giving results identical regardless worker count backend. wires directly seed arguments proposed stochastic wrappers §12 (item 7): passing seed seeds L’Ecuyer stream, study reproducible whether runs 1 core 64. document prominently test (seed ⇒ identical changepoints plan(sequential) plan(multisession)).

title: ggchangepoint: A Roadmap for the Next Release
text: Opt-, sequential default. Respects CRAN (examples/tests/vignettes capped two cores via _R_CHECK_LIMIT_CORES_), keeps casual use simple, avoids nested-parallelism blow-ups calls sit inside user’s future_map(). Parallelise outer, coarse loop. Distributing methods, series, replications gives high compute--overhead ratios; avoid parallelising tiny inner loops process/transfer overhead dominates. Overhead awareness. problem-size threshold stay sequential automatically; chunking (future.chunk.size) load-balancing absorb heterogeneous per-method runtimes ggcpt_compare() (fast PELT beside slow permutation test). Thread/process hygiene. Avoid oversubscription pairing multithreaded BLAS many forked workers unguarded (document RhpcBLASctl::blas_set_num_threads() HD methods): multithreading lives C++ engines/BLAS, multiprocessing future layer.
